news_summary: Recent discoveries and initiatives highlight cooperation and reconciliation, revealing Neanderthals and Homo sapiens collaborated 110,000 years ago and women ex-combatants in Colombia are fostering peace through creative empowerment.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes et des initiatives récentes mettent en avant la coopération et la réconciliation, révélant que les Néandertaliens et les Homo sapiens ont collaboré il y a 110 000 ans et que des femmes anciennes combattantes en Colombie œuvrent pour la paix grâce à l'autonomisation par la création.
news_summary_es: Los últimos descubrimientos e iniciativas ponen de relieve la cooperación y la reconciliación, revelando que los neandertales y el Homo sapiens colaboraron hace 110 000 años y que las mujeres excombatientes en Colombia están promoviendo la paz a través del empoderamiento creativo.

articles.title: 110,000-year-old discovery rewrites human history: Neanderthals and Homo sapiens worked together
articles.summary: The first-ever published research on Tinshemet Cave reveals that Neanderthals and Homo sapiens in the mid-Middle Paleolithic Levant not only coexisted but actively interacted, sharing technology, lifestyles, and burial customs. These interactions fostered cultural exchange, social complexity, and behavioral innovations, such as formal burial practices and the symbolic use of ochre for decoration. The findings suggest that human connections, rather than isolation, were key drivers of technological and cultural advancements, highlighting the Levant as a crucial crossroads in early human history.
articles.content:
The first-ever published research on Tinshemet Cave is changing how scientists understand the relationship between Neanderthals and Homo sapiens. Evidence from the site shows that these groups did more than simply live at the same time in the mid-Middle Paleolithic Levant. They interacted directly, sharing tools, ways of life, and even burial practices. These exchanges appear to have encouraged cultural growth, more complex social behavior, and innovations such as formal burials and the symbolic use of ochre for decoration. The findings point to human interaction, rather than isolation, as a key force behind early technological and cultural progress, with the Levant acting as a major crossroads in human history.

Located in central Israel, Tinshemet Cave has produced an exceptional collection of archaeological and human remains. Among the most important discoveries are several human burials, the first mid-Middle Palaeolithic (MP) burials uncovered in more than fifty years. These finds are offering rare insight into how early human groups lived and treated their dead.

The study, published in Nature Human Behaviour, is the first to present results from the site. It provides strong evidence that Neanderthals and Homo sapiens not only shared the region but also influenced each other's daily activities, technologies, and rituals. This challenges earlier ideas that viewed these groups as largely separate and instead suggests a much closer and more complex relationship.

Scientists Investigate Neanderthal and Human Relationships

Excavations at Tinshemet Cave began in 2017 and are led by Prof. Yossi Zaidner of the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Prof. Israel Hershkovitz of Tel Aviv University, and Dr. Marion Prévost of the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. One of the central questions guiding the research is how Neanderthals and Homo sapiens interacted during the mid-Middle Palaeolithic in this region. Researchers are exploring whether these groups competed, coexisted peacefully, or worked together in meaningful ways.

To answer this, the team examined evidence across four main areas: stone tool production, hunting strategies, symbolic behavior, and social complexity. Their analysis suggests that multiple human groups, including Neanderthals, pre-Neanderthals, and Homo sapiens, maintained ongoing contact. These interactions allowed ideas and skills to spread, gradually making different groups more culturally similar over time.

Shared Rituals and Early Symbolism

The research also points to important behavioral developments driven by these connections. Around 110,000 years ago, formal burial practices began to appear in Israel for the first time anywhere in the world. This shift may reflect stronger social ties and shared traditions among groups.

One striking feature of the cave is the widespread use of mineral pigments, especially ochre. Researchers believe it may have been used to decorate bodies, possibly serving as a way to express identity or distinguish between groups. This kind of symbolic behavior suggests a deeper level of social meaning than previously recognized.

Was Tinshemet Cave an Ancient Cemetery

The arrangement of burials at the site raises the possibility that Tinshemet Cave served as a dedicated burial area or even an early cemetery. If that is the case, it would point to organized rituals and strong community bonds. Objects placed alongside the dead, including stone tools, animal bones, and pieces of ochre, may also hint at early beliefs about an afterlife.

A Crossroads of Human Evolution

Prof. Zaidner describes the region as a "melting pot" where different human populations came together and influenced one another. "Our data show that human connections and population interactions have been fundamental in driving cultural and technological innovations throughout history," he explains.

Dr. Prévost emphasizes the importance of geography in shaping these interactions. "During the mid-MP, climatic improvements increased the region's carrying capacity, leading to demographic expansion and intensified contact between different Homo taxa."

Prof. Hershkovitz highlights how closely linked these groups may have been. "These findings paint a picture of dynamic interactions shaped by both cooperation and competition."

New Insights Into Early Human Society

The discoveries at Tinshemet Cave provide a detailed look at the social lives, behaviors, and environments of early humans. They reveal a time of major population growth and cultural change, driven by contact between different human groups. As research at the site continues, scientists expect even more discoveries that could deepen our understanding of how human society first began to take shape.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that reshapes understanding of early human history by showing cooperation and cultural exchange between Neanderthals and Homo sapiens. This has broad implications for human origins and social development, highlighting the importance of connection and shared culture in human evolution. The story is focused, detailed, and presents a meaningful breakthrough with wide societal and educational impact.
articles.personality_title: New discovery shows Neanderthals and early humans lived and worked together
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Around 110,000 years ago, two different human groups, Neanderthals and Homo sapiens, lived in the Levant region, which is now part of Israel and nearby areas. Scientists used to think these groups mostly lived apart or competed with each other.

**What happened** – Researchers studying Tinshemet Cave in Israel found evidence that Neanderthals and Homo sapiens didn’t just live near each other but actually shared tools, lifestyles, and burial customs. Excavations starting in 2017 uncovered human burials, stone tools, and ochre decorations showing they influenced each other. This research was published in the journal Nature Human Behaviour.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how we understand early human history. It shows these groups cooperated and exchanged ideas, which helped them develop new behaviors like formal burials and symbolic use of colors. The site might have been an early cemetery, suggesting strong social ties and shared beliefs. It also highlights the Levant as a key meeting place for human evolution.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue digging and studying Tinshemet Cave to learn more about how these early humans lived and interacted. Future findings may reveal even more about the origins of human culture and social life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Tinshemet Cave discovery reveals that Neanderthals and Homo sapiens cooperated and shared culture, reshaping ideas about early human history.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une découverte montre que Néandertaliens et premiers humains vivaient et travaillaient ensemble
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a environ 110 000 ans, deux groupes humains différents, les Néandertaliens et les Homo sapiens, vivaient dans la région du Levant, qui correspond aujourd’hui à Israël et ses environs. Les scientifiques pensaient auparavant que ces groupes vivaient surtout séparés ou étaient en compétition.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs étudiant la grotte de Tinshemet en Israël ont trouvé des preuves que Néandertaliens et Homo sapiens ne se contentaient pas de vivre près les uns des autres, mais partageaient aussi des outils, des modes de vie et des coutumes funéraires. Les fouilles commencées en 2017 ont mis au jour des sépultures humaines, des outils en pierre et des décorations à l’ocre montrant qu’ils s’influençaient mutuellement. Ces recherches ont été publiées dans la revue Nature Human Behaviour.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension de l’histoire humaine ancienne. Elle montre que ces groupes coopéraient et échangeaient des idées, ce qui a aidé à développer des comportements nouveaux comme les enterrements formels et l’usage symbolique des couleurs. Le site pourrait avoir été un cimetière ancien, suggérant des liens sociaux forts et des croyances partagées. Elle souligne aussi le Levant comme un lieu clé de rencontre dans l’évolution humaine.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à fouiller et étudier la grotte de Tinshemet pour en apprendre davantage sur la vie et les interactions de ces premiers humains. De futures découvertes pourraient révéler encore plus sur les origines de la culture et de la vie sociale humaines.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La découverte de la grotte de Tinshemet montre que Néandertaliens et Homo sapiens ont coopéré et partagé leur culture, transformant notre regard sur l’histoire humaine ancienne.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo descubrimiento muestra que neandertales y primeros humanos vivían y trabajaban juntos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace unos 110,000 años, dos grupos humanos diferentes, los neandertales y Homo sapiens, vivían en la región del Levante, que hoy es parte de Israel y áreas cercanas. Antes, los científicos pensaban que estos grupos vivían separados o competían entre sí.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores que estudiaron la cueva Tinshemet en Israel encontraron pruebas de que neandertales y Homo sapiens no solo vivían cerca, sino que compartían herramientas, estilos de vida y costumbres funerarias. Las excavaciones que comenzaron en 2017 revelaron entierros humanos, herramientas de piedra y decoraciones con ocre que muestran que se influían mutuamente. Esta investigación se publicó en la revista Nature Human Behaviour.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia cómo entendemos la historia humana antigua. Muestra que estos grupos cooperaban y compartían ideas, lo que les ayudó a desarrollar nuevos comportamientos como entierros formales y el uso simbólico de colores. El sitio pudo ser un cementerio temprano, lo que sugiere vínculos sociales fuertes y creencias compartidas. También destaca al Levante como un lugar clave en la evolución humana.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos continuarán excavando y estudiando la cueva Tinshemet para aprender más sobre cómo vivían e interactuaban estos primeros humanos. Los futuros descubrimientos podrían revelar aún más sobre los orígenes de la cultura y la vida social humanas.

**Resumen en una frase** – El descubrimiento en la cueva Tinshemet muestra que neandertales y Homo sapiens cooperaron y compartieron cultura, cambiando las ideas sobre la historia humana antigua.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two intertwined ancient stone tools—one shaped like a Neanderthal hand axe and the other a Homo sapiens blade—resting gently beside ochre-stained bones arranged as a symbolic burial in a softly glowing cave interior, with subtle earth tones and natural pigments highlighting shared culture and cooperation.

articles.title: From conflict to catwalk: Women ex-combatants weave reconciliation in Colombia
articles.summary: Katerine Avella is a former combatant in Colombia’s decades-long civil war, a peace signatory and a community leader. After the guns fell silent, she created the fashion brand Ixora but, with violence returning to the region, Ms. Avella is now focusing on trying to keep the project afloat in the face of new challenges.
articles.content:
Peace must be sewn, stitch by stitch.

With this idea in mind, Ms. Avella set herself to work at a small sewing workshop in Catatumbo, Colombia, one of the dividends of the 2016 peace agreement between the Colombian Government and FARC rebels, designed to help reintegrate former combatants, and heal the wounds of the conflict.

Like many former fighters, she was looking for a way to rebuild her life after the war, and the workshop served as a space for training, care and empowerment for women that would contribute to preventing gender-based violence – a place where they could learn a trade, support each other and regain confidence in the midst of a territory marked by violence.

Along with several other women, Ms. Avella set up Stitches for Peace, which began making sweatshirts, T-shirts and uniforms. But in 2021 the project took an unexpected turn towards high fashion.

At an initiative led by the UN Verification Mission in Colombia, she met Lina Garcés, an economist trained at the Externado University and the founder of a second-hand clothing boutique called Lina's Closet, in Cúcuta.

Ms. Garcés used to say that her shop sold “second-chance clothes,” a phrase that would soon take on a new meaning.

Ms. Garcés agreed to participate, although not without reservations. Her personal history was marked by the armed conflict, and her family had been victims of a kidnapping, which left painful memories.

However, she decided to travel to Caño Indio, in the middle of the Catatumbo jungle, where she found a place very different from the fashion world in which she worked: prefabricated accommodations, zinc roofs, unpaved roads and communal bathrooms.

But she also found something she did not expect: talent. “The women had an impressive ability,” she recalls. “The one who sewed did it with incredible precision; the one who cut had the pulse of a professional.” Many had learned to handle needle and thread during the war, mending uniforms or boots. Now that knowledge wove another story.

UNVMC Katerine Avella and Lina Garcés celebrate the creation of the Ixora brand’s skirts.

The skirts of Ixora and reconciliation

For fifteen days they worked intensely on designs, sizing and finishes. From those days was born the idea of creating wraparound skirts with prints inspired by the Ixora flower, a plant that blooms all year round and symbolizes resistance and perseverance in Catatumbo.

As a result of all the workshops, the brand 'Ixora, inclusive and autonomous' was born. By the end of 2021, they already had a first collection, which they presented at the Julio Pérez library in Cúcuta. The parade brought together victims of the conflict and peace signatories on the same catwalk.

Sometime later, during a discussion at the Cúcuta Book Fair, where they were invited to tell their story, Ms. Garcés told her family history for the first time before the public. As she spoke, Ms. Avella listened to her in silence. Ms. Garcés said in front of the audience: “For me, today they are sensitive women, who want to move forward. For my part, there was forgiveness; now I want to support them and let more people know their work so that we can live in peace.”

The skirts began to be sold through Ms. Garcés's store and soon other designers were interested in the initiative. Ixora began appearing in fashion shows in Tibú, Ocaña and Bogotá, and in 2022 they arrived for the first time at Colombiamoda, the most important textile fair in the country. They did so again in 2023 and 2024, as special guests, on the catwalk.

UNVMC Women from the Ixora brand at the trade show following the conclusion of a garment-making workshop organized by UNVMC

Violence returns

However, this story of reconciliation, entrepreneurship and peace was strongly affected in January last year, when violence intensified again in this region. There were massive displacements, murders of social leaders and ex-combatants, and thousands of families were forced to leave their homes.

The sewing workshop had to close. “The women didn't want to go back out of fear,” Ms. Avella recalls. At that time, the priority was to protect life.

Paradoxically, in the midst of that crisis came news that they had been waiting for months: Ixora had been officially registered as a trademark in Colombia by the Superintendence of Industry and Commerce. But they decided not to celebrate. “It wasn't the time," Ms. Avella says. "There was too much uncertainty.”

Today the project is going through a forced pause. The sewing machines are still in Caño Indio, while the women are waiting for guarantees to be able to move to a new, more secure space in a rural area of Cúcuta.

Hope for the future

Even so, the story of Ixora is not over. The brand has just won a project with the Agency for Reincorporation and Normalization (ARN) to accompany other women in self-care and psychological support processes. It is a new stage for Ixora, which now seeks not only to generate income, but also to offer a space of well-being for those who have lived through the impacts of the conflict.

“This project is a dream,” says Ms. Avella serenely. “Beyond the economic side, it means keeping our association alive and showing that we can build something different.”

In the meantime, the women wait for the moment to turn the machines back on. In Catatumbo, where so many stories end abruptly, Like the flower that gives it its name, there is hope that Ixora will bloom again, even in the most difficult conditions.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful peacebuilding and reintegration initiative in Colombia where former women combatants use fashion as a tool for reconciliation, empowerment, and economic independence. This project has broad social significance by promoting healing after conflict, supporting victims and ex-combatants, and fostering community resilience. Despite recent setbacks due to renewed violence, the initiative continues to offer hope and psychological support, demonstrating substantial positive real-world impact with detailed context.
articles.personality_title: Former fighters in Colombia create fashion brand to promote peace and healing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Colombia experienced a long civil war that lasted many years. In 2016, a peace agreement was signed between the government and rebel groups like FARC. This agreement included programs to help former fighters return to normal life and heal communities affected by violence.

**What happened** – Katerine Avella, a former combatant and peace agreement signatory, started a sewing workshop in Catatumbo, Colombia. Along with other women, she created the brand Ixora, making clothes like skirts inspired by a flower symbolizing strength and hope. The brand grew, appearing at major fashion events and bringing together people affected by the conflict. However, violence returned in 2023, forcing the workshop to close and the women to pause their work.

**Impact** – Ixora is more than a fashion brand; it helps women who lived through violence to gain skills, confidence, and income. It also brings together victims and former fighters, promoting understanding and peace. The project shows how creativity and teamwork can help heal deep wounds in a community.

**What's next step** – Although the workshop is temporarily closed, Ixora won a new project to support women’s mental health and well-being. The women hope to reopen the workshop in a safer place soon and continue their work of making clothes and building peace.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Former fighters in Colombia are using fashion to rebuild their lives, promote peace, and support each other despite ongoing challenges.
articles.personality_title_fr: D'anciennes combattantes en Colombie créent une marque de mode pour promouvoir la paix et la guérison
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Colombie a connu une longue guerre civile qui a duré de nombreuses années. En 2016, un accord de paix a été signé entre le gouvernement et des groupes rebelles comme les FARC. Cet accord incluait des programmes pour aider les anciens combattants à retrouver une vie normale et à guérir les communautés touchées par la violence.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Katerine Avella, ancienne combattante et signataire de l’accord de paix, a lancé un atelier de couture à Catatumbo, en Colombie. Avec d’autres femmes, elle a créé la marque Ixora, fabriquant des vêtements comme des jupes inspirées d’une fleur symbolisant la force et l’espoir. La marque a grandi, participant à des événements de mode importants et réunissant des victimes et d’anciens combattants. Mais la violence est revenue en 2023, obligeant l’atelier à fermer et les femmes à suspendre leur travail.

**Impact** – Ixora est plus qu’une marque de mode ; elle aide des femmes ayant vécu la violence à acquérir des compétences, de la confiance et un revenu. Elle réunit aussi victimes et anciens combattants, favorisant la compréhension et la paix. Ce projet montre comment la créativité et le travail d’équipe peuvent guérir des blessures profondes dans une communauté.

**Prochaine étape** – Bien que l’atelier soit temporairement fermé, Ixora a remporté un nouveau projet pour soutenir la santé mentale et le bien-être des femmes. Les femmes espèrent rouvrir l’atelier dans un lieu plus sûr bientôt et poursuivre leur travail de confection et de construction de la paix.

**Résumé en une phrase** – D’anciennes combattantes en Colombie utilisent la mode pour reconstruire leur vie, promouvoir la paix et se soutenir malgré les défis.
articles.personality_title_es: Excombatientes en Colombia crean marca de moda para promover la paz y la sanación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Colombia vivió una larga guerra civil que duró muchos años. En 2016 se firmó un acuerdo de paz entre el gobierno y grupos rebeldes como las FARC. Este acuerdo incluye programas para ayudar a los excombatientes a volver a la vida normal y sanar comunidades afectadas por la violencia.

**Qué pasó** – Katerine Avella, excombatiente y firmante de la paz, comenzó un taller de costura en Catatumbo, Colombia. Junto a otras mujeres, creó la marca Ixora, haciendo ropa como faldas inspiradas en una flor que simboliza fuerza y esperanza. La marca creció, participando en eventos de moda importantes y reuniendo a víctimas y excombatientes. Pero en 2023 volvió la violencia, y el taller tuvo que cerrar, deteniendo su trabajo.

**Impacto** – Ixora es más que una marca de moda; ayuda a mujeres que vivieron violencia a aprender habilidades, ganar confianza y obtener ingresos. También une a víctimas y excombatientes, promoviendo el entendimiento y la paz. Este proyecto muestra cómo la creatividad y el trabajo en equipo pueden sanar heridas profundas en una comunidad.

**Próximo paso** – Aunque el taller está cerrado temporalmente, Ixora ganó un nuevo proyecto para apoyar la salud mental y el bienestar de las mujeres. Esperan reabrir el taller en un lugar más seguro pronto y continuar su trabajo de confección y construcción de paz.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Excombatientes en Colombia usan la moda para reconstruir sus vidas, promover la paz y apoyarse pese a los desafíos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vibrant Ixora flower blooming amidst colorful woven fabrics and sewing tools arranged like a peaceful workshop in a rural Colombian setting, with soft natural tones and gentle light symbolizing resilience, reconciliation, and hope emerging from conflict.
